details.text: Derivative: شِيَعِ (Shiya’) is the plural of شِيعَة (Shee’ah) is one of the words used to refer to “a group” and it implies a group that you draw strength from by joining it. It comes from شَاعَ which means to spread and become known and can imply a group that spreads and becomes known. It is used commonly to refer to a group that follow, support or are unified upon a particular person, Madh’hab or issue.
Derivative: نَسْلُكُ comes from سَلَكَ – يَسْلُكُ – سَلْك which carries from among its meanings the meaning of “to insert” something into something.

Derivative: ظَلُّوا is from ظَلَّ – يَظَلُّ and is used to imply constancy and continuance. It is used in Arabic usually to refer to something that is continued to be done during the day as opposed to بَاتَ which is used to something that is continued to be done during the night.

- “Our eyes (أَبْصَارُنَا) have only (إِنَّمَا) become intoxicated (سُكِّرَتْ)” – This means that they are saying: “We are only seeing illusions or delusions” i.e., they refuse to believe what their own eyes are seeing and make excuses for their refusal.

Derivative: بُرُوجًا (Burooj) is the plural of بُرْج (Burj) and comes from بَرَجَ – يَبْرُجُ which combines the meanings of being visible or apparent and elevated or high. بُرُوجًا (Burooj) is used to refer to “towers, constellations and stars”.

Derivative: رَجِيمٍ (Rajeem) comes from رَجَمَ – يَرْجُمُ – رَجْم which means “to stone” and “to curse”. رَجِيمٍ (Rajeem) is in the فَعِيل (Fa’eel) form with the meaning of the مَفْعُول (Maf’ool) form. رَجِيمٍ (Rajeem) therefore means مَرْجُوم (Marjoom) which means “To be stoned or pelted with stones” or “To be cursed”.
Derivative: اسْتَرَقَ means “to steal or sneak something away”.
Derivative: شِهَابٌ (Shihaab) refers to something that is a bright flame of fire. It is used to refer to shooting stars and meteors.
Derivative: مَعَايِش (Ma’aayish) is the plural of مَعِيشَة (Ma’eeshah) and refers to “means of life. livelihood and sustenance”.

Derivative: لَوَاقِحَ (Lawaaqih) is the plural of لَاقِحَة (Laahiqah). It comes from لَقَحَ – يَلْقَحُ – لَقْح which linguistically means to put something inside of something. لَاقِح (Laaqih) was originally used to refer to a she camel that was inseminated and became pregnant. It is used to mean “to pollinate and impregnate”. Here in reference to the winds, it is used as if the winds are pregnant. This either implies that the winds are pregnant with clouds or pollen i.e., full of the sources of life. It can also imply that the clouds are pregnant with or carrying water in the case where the winds are being used to refer to the clouds themselves.

Derivative: مَّسْنُونٍ (Masnoon) comes from سَنَّ that can hold very meanings. Meanings which have been given here include “That which been left for a period of time” in the case where سُنَّة (Sunnah) refers to a trend or norm that is established over time. It has also been said to refer to “That which is putrid and foul smelling” because it has been left for a long time. Lastly it has also been said to refer to something that has been “moulded, formed and shaped”.
Derivative: حَمَإٍ (Hama’in) refers to very dark or near black mud due to being mixed with water and has a changed smell to it.

Derivative: السَّمُومِ (Samoom) refers to a scorching hot wind. It is called as such because the intensity of its heat and its effect penetrates the pores of the body’s skin which are called مَسامّ (Masaamm).
Derivative: يُلْهِ is from أَلْهَى – يُلْهِي – إِلْهَاء which means “to distract”.
Derivative: الْأَمَلُ (Amal) means “hope” and it refers here to long-term hopes related to the Dunyaa (worldly life).
Derivative: أَجْمَعُونَ is the اِسْمُ التَفْضِيل (Ism ut-Tafdeel – Superlative) form which is more powerful and has greater emphasis than جَمِيعًا (Jamee’) when stating the meaning of “All of them”.

Derivative: أُغْوِي is from أَغْوَى which means to take somebody far off or away from the path where he is unable to find his way back. لَأُغْوِيَنَّ – The additional لَام (Lam) and نُون (Noon) attached to the beginning and end of the word adds three levels of emphasis (تَوْكِيد -Tawkeed) to the word providing the meaning: “I will assuredly without doubt lead them astray”.
Derivative: كِتَابٌ (Kitaab) here refers to a time that has been written or specified for them like the meaning of أَجَل (Ajal).

Derivative: الْمُخْلَصِينَ is the plural of مُخْلَص (Mukhlas) which is an اِسْم مَفْعُول (Ism Maf’ool – Passive participle) and implies the meaning of the “one who has been made sincere or been granted sincerity” in contrast to مُخْلِص (Mukhlis) which is an اِسْم فَاعِل (Ism Faa’il – Active participle) and means the “one who is sincere”.

Derivative: عَلَىَّ is composed of عَلَى and ي. It can imply the meaning of “upon me” in terms of it being mandated upon Me or I have taken it upon myself i.e., to make clear. Or it can carry the meaning of إِلِيَّ (Ilayya) which means “to me”.
Derivative: الْغَاوِينَ comes from غَوَى and is the plural of غَاوٍ (Ghaawin) which refers to “the one who strays from the path”.
Derivative: نَصَبٌ (Nasab) comes from نَصِبَ – يَنْصَبُ which means to exert oneself to the point of exhaustion. نَصَبٌ (Nasab) refers to that “exertion and fatigue”.
Derivative: تَسْبِقُ comes from سَبَقَ which means “to precede” and here it means “to advance or bring forward” in the context of: No nation can “bring forward” its specified deadline.
Derivative: الْقَانِطِينَ is the plural of قَانِط (Qaanit) which is the active participle from قَنَطَ – يَقْنُطُ – قُنُوط which means to despair, to be despondent and lose hope.

Derivative: خَطْبُ (Khatb) refers to a matter, concern or situation that entails some trouble or danger. مَا خَطْبُكَ “What’s your Khatb” implies “What's your issue, business, agenda or affair” i.e., what are you up to?
Derivative: الْغَابِرِينَ is the plural of غَابِر (Ghaabir) which means “the one who remains or is left behind”.
Derivative: مُّنكَرُونَ is the plural of مُنْكَر (Munkar) which means “unknown or unrecognised” and is the opposite of مَعْرُوف (Ma’roof) which means “known or recognised”.
Derivative: امْضُوا is the plural command form from مَضَى – يَمْضِي – مُضِيًّا which means “to keep on moving or to proceed, walk and continue on”.

Derivative: “The root (دَابِرَ) of those (هَٰؤُلَاءِ) will be cut off (مَقْطُوعٌ)” – قَطْعُ دَابِر الشَّيْء “To cut the Daabir (root) of the thing” is an expression in Arabic that means “to eradicate it”.
Derivative: تَفْضَحُون is from فَضَحَ – يَفْضَحُ – فَضْحًا which means to expose, shame, dishonour and bring disgrace to someone.
- لَّوْ (Law) and مَا (Maa) combined means the same as هَلا (Halaa) which implies the meaning of “Why didn’t or wouldn’t” you do such and such a thing. Here it means: “Why don’t (you bring) …”
- “By your life span” – This reflects a unique oath which is sworn by Allah upon the lifespan (عَمْر) of the Messenger ﷺ.

Derivative: مُشْرِقِينَ comes from أَشْرَقَ which means for “the sun to rise”. It combines the sun rising with the active participle form to provide the meaning of “when they entered into the time of sunrise” or “as they were rising and sunrise”.

Derivative: مُتَوَسِّمِينَ is the plural of مُتَوَسِّم (Mutawassim). وَسْم (Wasm) means “a mark” and تَوَسَّمَ means to learn by looking at the marks and signs. It implies the meaning of “to scrutinise” something and “examine it carefully”. The مُتَوَسِّم (Mutawassim) is the person “who scrutinises and studies something carefully” to understand it or reach conclusions about it.
Derivative: سَبِيلٍ (Sabeel) refers “to a path or road that is easily frequently travelled upon”.
Derivative: إِمَامٍ (Imaam) here refers to a road that is clear in its features. The road is called Imaam because the people are led and guided by it.

- أَصْحَابُ الْحِجْرِ “The people (أَصْحَابُ) of the rock formations (الْحِجْرِ)” refers to Thamud the Qawm (nation) of Salih (as). الْحِجْرِ (Al-Hijr) was said to be the name of the place that they lived in and it is used to describe any place surrounded by rocks.
Derivative: اصْفَحِ comes from صَفَحَ – يَصْفَحُ – صَفْح which means “to overlook” and “forgive”.

Derivative: الْخَلَّاقُ is in the hyperbole form (صِيغَةُ المُبَالَغَة – Seeghat ul-Mubaalaghah) of الخَالِق (Al-Khaaliq) which means “The Creator”. It indicates to the vastness and abundance of His creation.

Derivative: سَبْعًا مِّنَ الْمَثَانِي “Seven of (سَبْعًا مِّنَ) Al-Mathaaniy (الْمَثَانِي)” – The majority opinion is that this refers to Surah Al-Fatihah. الْمَثَانِي (Al-Mathaaniy) has been said to come from تَثْنِيَة (Tathniyah) which means “repetition” in which case it is understood to imply “The seven oft-repeated verses”. And it has also been understood to come from ثَنَاء (Thanaa’) which means “praise or extolled” in which case it is understood to imply “The seven highly praised verses”.

Derivative: أَزْوَاجًا (Azwaaj) is the plural of زَوْج (Zawj) which is used to refer to “one of a pair”. It is also used in Arabic to mean صَنْف (Sanf) the plural of which is أَصْنَاف (Asnaaf) which refers to “a category, kind or type” of something. The meaning here is أَصْنَاف (Asnaaf) referring to particular “Categories” from among the disbelievers.
- “Lower (اخْفِضْ) your wing (جَنَاحَكَ)” – It is an expression of manifesting humility, affection and leniency.
Derivative: حَافِظُونَ (Haafizhoon) comes from حِفْظ (Hifzh) which means to “protect, preserve and safeguard”. حَافِظ (Haafizh) is the one who “protects, preserves and safeguards” something.
Derivative: عِضِينَ (‘Ideen) means “into parts or categories” in the case where they would accept some parts of the Qur’an and leave or ignore others.

Derivative: اصْدَعْ comes from صَدَعَ – يَصْدَع – صَدْعًا which means to breakthrough obstacles and challenges to accomplish something. It can therefore imply the meaning here of to persevere even though it is difficult. It has also been said to mean “to proclaim and declare openly” in reference to the Da’wah (invitation and call). الصَّدْع بالحَقِّ is used in Arabic to mean “To proclaim the truth”.

- “Your chest (صَدْرُكَ) is constrained or tightened (يَضِيقُ)” – ضِيقُ الصَّدْر (Deequ s-Sadr) “The constriction of the chest” is an expression that refers to being exposed to worries and sorrows. It is used to describe the emotional burden that can weigh down the heart.
Derivative: الْيَقِينُ (Al-Yaqeen) means “the certainty” and is a metaphor for death.
